title: Medicare Part B Premium 2026: What You'll Pay and How to Choose the Right Coverage
rawContent:
[Generated from outline]
Keyword: medicare plan b premium 2026
Content Type: blog

sections.text: Understanding Your Medicare Part B Premium for 2026

sections.content: The standard Medicare Part B premium for 2026 is **$202.90 per month**, up from $185 in 2025. That increase reflects rising health care costs across the system, not something tied to your personal spending.

This number matters more than it might seem at first glance. Your Part B premium is a fixed monthly cost that affects how you budget for the rest of your coverage, whether you pair it with a Medicare Supplement plan or a Medicare Advantage plan.
This article breaks down exactly what drives your 2026 premium, where extra costs can appear, and how to compare your coverage options with a clear head instead of guesswork.

sections.calloutText: The standard 2026 Part B premium is $202.90 per month. Your actual amount may be higher depending on your income from your 2024 tax return.

sections.text: Medicare Terms and Costs Made Simple: Breaking Down Plan B in Plain Language

sections.content: Medicare Part B covers outpatient medical care. That includes doctor visits, lab work, preventive screenings, and durable medical equipment. It works alongside Part A, which covers hospital stays, and it's separate from Part D, which handles prescription drugs, and Medicare Advantage, which bundles several types of coverage into one plan.

Many people mix up these terms early in their Medicare research. Understanding that Part B is specifically your outpatient medical coverage, not your hospital or drug coverage, makes every other decision easier to follow.

The 2026 standard premium of $202.90 is a noticeable jump from the 2025 amount of $185. Premiums rarely stay flat year to year because they're tied to projected health spending and program costs across the country.

sections.title: Part B Costs: 2025 vs. 2026
sections.footnote: Figures reflect standard amounts before any income-related adjustments.

sections.content: Beyond the premium, you're responsible for the annual Part B deductible, which rises to $283 in 2026. After you meet that deductible, Medicare typically pays 80% of approved costs for covered services, leaving you responsible for the remaining 20% unless you have supplemental coverage in place.

sections.calloutText: Think of your Part B premium as just one piece of your total cost picture. The deductible and 20% coinsurance can add up quickly without a Medigap or Medicare Advantage plan to help absorb them.

sections.text: What Drives Your Actual Costs: Premiums, IRMAA, and Out-of-Pocket Surprises

sections.content: Not everyone pays the standard premium. If your income is above certain thresholds, you'll pay an Income-Related Monthly Adjustment Amount, known as IRMAA, added on top of the base premium. This adjustment is based on your tax return from two years earlier, so your 2026 premium reflects your 2024 income.

For example, an individual filer with income above $109,000 but below $137,000 would move into IRMAA Level 2, paying $284.10 per month instead of the standard $202.90. Someone with income above $500,000 lands in Level 6, paying $689.90 per month for Part B alone.

sections.title: 2026 IRMAA Part B Monthly Premiums (Individual Filers)
sections.footnote: Joint filers and those married filing separately have different thresholds. Figures based on 2024 income reported to the IRS.

sections.content: Your total cost also depends on how you pair Part B with other coverage. A Medicare Supplement plan can cover the 20% coinsurance Part B leaves behind, while a Medicare Advantage plan bundles your Part B benefits with a yearly out-of-pocket maximum, capped at $9,250 in 2026.

If the premium feels like a financial stretch, you may qualify for a Medicare Savings Program that helps pay Part B costs based on income and resources. Contacting your state Medicaid office or a SHIP counselor is a reasonable first step to check eligibility.

sections.calloutText: IRMAA is based on your income from two years prior, so a one-time income spike, like a home sale or retirement account withdrawal, could temporarily raise your premium. You can request a reconsideration if your income has since dropped due to a life-changing event.

sections.content: I always tell people not to panic over the sticker price of Part B alone. The bigger question is how it works with the rest of your coverage, because a plan with a low premium but weak supplemental protection can end up costing you far more when you actually need care. Look at your full picture before you decide.

sections.text: Will Your Doctors and Prescriptions Still Be Covered Under Part B?

sections.content: Part B pays for outpatient visits, preventive screenings like annual wellness exams, and medically necessary durable medical equipment such as wheelchairs or oxygen equipment. It generally covers 80% of the Medicare-approved amount once you've met your deductible.

What Part B does not cover is most self-administered prescription drugs. That's the job of Part D or the drug coverage bundled into many Medicare Advantage plans. If your medication list is long, this distinction affects your planning significantly.

items: Outpatient doctor visits and specialist consultations
Preventive care, including screenings and vaccines
Durable medical equipment like walkers or nebulizers

sections.content: Before finalizing any coverage decision, confirm that your current doctors accept Medicare assignment. This means they agree to accept the Medicare-approved amount as full payment, which protects you from being billed more than your expected share.

Pairing Part B with the right supplemental coverage, whether that's a Medigap plan or a Medicare Advantage plan with strong provider networks, helps shield you from the unpredictable bills that come with the 20% coinsurance Part B leaves uncovered.

sections.calloutText: You can verify whether a specific doctor accepts Medicare assignment through the Medicare.gov physician lookup tool before you commit to a plan.

sections.text: Avoiding Costly Mistakes: Enrollment Deadlines and Late Penalties You Need to Know

sections.content: Your Initial Enrollment Period runs for seven months: the three months before your 65th birthday month, your birthday month, and the three months after. Missing this window without qualifying coverage elsewhere can lead to a permanent penalty.
If you miss your Initial Enrollment Period, the General Enrollment Period runs from January 1 to March 31 each year, with coverage typically starting the month after you enroll.

The Part B late enrollment penalty adds 10% to your premium for each full 12-month period you were eligible but didn't enroll. This penalty is added to your premium for as long as you have Part B, which for most people means for life.

sections.calloutText: The Part B late enrollment penalty is not a one-time fee. It's added to your monthly premium indefinitely, making it one of the most expensive Medicare mistakes to avoid.

sections.content: Delaying Part B can make sense if you have active employer coverage through your own job or a spouse's job at a company with 20 or more employees. In that case, you typically qualify for a Special Enrollment Period once that coverage ends.

If you've already missed a deadline and are worried about a penalty, don't assume there's nothing you can do. Speaking with a SHIP counselor or a licensed agent can help you understand your specific situation and whether any exceptions apply.

sections.text: How to Compare Plans With Confidence Instead of Guessing Alone

sections.content: A useful comparison starts with three questions: What will this cost me monthly and out-of-pocket? Are my doctors included? Are my prescriptions covered at a reasonable cost? Working through these in order keeps you from getting distracted by premium alone.

Turning 65 soon, or receiving Medicare enrollment paperwork in the mail, is often the moment people realize they need to make a real decision rather than keep researching indefinitely. That's a good trigger to start comparing specific plans available in your area rather than general information.

items: List your current doctors and medications
Compare your premium, deductible, and expected coinsurance across plan types
Check whether your doctors accept Medicare assignment
Review prescription formularies for your specific medications
Ask a licensed agent to walk through your options side by side

sections.content: Licensed Medicare agents are trained to explain your options in plain language, focused on your specific needs rather than pushing a particular plan. A transparent, needs-based comparison process should walk through your doctors, prescriptions, and budget before recommending anything.

sections.calloutText: Real client reviews and testimonials can help you evaluate whether a source or agent is genuinely working in your interest before you commit to a conversation.

sections.text: Frequently Asked Questions About the 2026 Part B Premium

faqs.question: I feel like I need more time before I talk to anyone. Is that okay?
faqs.answer: Yes, there's no rush to speak with anyone outside your actual enrollment deadlines. While you take your time, it helps to gather your current doctor and prescription lists so you're ready to compare options once you decide to move forward.

faqs.question: Will I get a pushy sales call if I ask a question about my premium?
faqs.answer: Licensed agents are expected to educate first and answer only what you ask, not push you toward a decision. Reputable services are transparent about how the process works, and you stay in control of the conversation the entire time.

faqs.question: Can I just research this myself before making a decision?
faqs.answer: Self-research is a reasonable first step, and many people start there. Where it often gets complicated is with IRMAA brackets, plan-specific provider networks, and drug formularies, since those details vary by your income, location, and medications.

faqs.question: How do I know which sources about the 2026 premium I can trust?
faqs.answer: Cross-check any information against Medicare.gov or guidance from a licensed agent to confirm accuracy. Reviews and real client experiences can also help you gauge whether a source explains things clearly without pressure or hidden agendas.

faqs.question: What if I choose a plan and regret it later?
faqs.answer: Medicare gives you annual enrollment periods to make changes, and Medicare Advantage plans include trial rights in some situations that allow you to switch back. Comparing your needs carefully upfront reduces the odds you'll need to make a change later.

faqs.question: Does everyone pay the same Part B premium in 2026?
faqs.answer: Most people pay the standard $202.90 monthly premium, but higher income can raise that amount through IRMAA. Some people pay less than the standard amount through a Medicare Savings Program or Medicaid assistance if they qualify based on income and resources.

sections.text: Your Next Step Toward Choosing Coverage With Confidence

sections.content: Your 2026 Part B premium starts at $202.90 for most people, but your actual cost depends on your income, your deductible, and how you pair Part B with other coverage. IRMAA, late penalties, and provider networks all factor into what you'll really pay.
Clarity comes from comparing your specific needs, not just chasing the lowest number on a page. Your doctors, your medications, and your budget all deserve a place in that comparison.
When you're ready, reaching out to a licensed Medicare agent for a no-pressure, personalized comparison can help you move from uncertainty to a decision you feel good about.

The 2026 Part B annual deductible increases to $283, after which Medicare pays 80% of approved costs
Higher earners pay an IRMAA surcharge based on 2024 income, ranging up to $689.90 per month at the highest level
Medicare Advantage plans cap out-of-pocket costs at $9,250 in 2026, while Medigap plans can cover Part B coinsurance
Medicare Savings Programs may help eligible individuals cover Part B premium costs
